records.title: Visual Access to Natural Systems in Some Cases
records.summary: In some cases, providing visual access to existing natural systems is the easiest and most cost-effective approach.

evidence.quote: à In some cases, providing visual access to existing natural systems will be the easiest and most cost–effective approach.

records.title: Specify quantities based on intended function
records.summary: Quantities of natural material should be specified based on intended function (restore vs stimulate), and variability of materials and applications is recommended over high ratios of any one material.
constraints: avoid high ratios of any one material

evidence.quote: • Making natural materials readily visible. Quantities of a (natural) material should be specified based on intended function of the space (e.g., to restore versus stimulate), though a degree of variability of materials and applications is recommended over high ratios of any one material.

records.title: Prioritize biodiversity over acreage
records.summary: Prioritize biodiversity over acreage, area or quantity.

evidence.quote: Prioritize biodiversity over acreage, area or quantity.

records.title: Mystery
records.summary: The characteristics of mystery conditions are based on visual preference and perceived danger, and supported by research on pleasure responses to anticipatory situations. Mystery engenders a strong pleasure response within the brain that may be a similar mechanism to that of anticipation.

evidence.quote: [13] Mystery. The characteristics of mystery conditions are based on visual preference and perceived danger (Herzog & Bryce, 2007; Herzog & Kropscott, 2004; Nasar, & Fisher, 1993), and supported by research on pleasure responses to anticipatory situations (Salimpoor, Benovoy, Larcher et al., 2011; Ikemi, 2005; Blood & Zatorre, 2001). Mystery engenders a strong pleasure response within the brain that may be a similar mechanism to that of anticipation (Biederman, 2011), which is hypothesized to be an explanation for why listening to music is so pleasurable – in that we are guessing what may be around the corner (Blood & Zatorre, 2001; Salimpoor et al., 2011).

records.title: Enable exercise opportunities near green space
records.summary: Prioritize or enable exercise opportunities that are in proximity to green space.
constraints: exercise opportunities should be near green space

evidence.quote: Prioritize or enable exercise opportunities that are in proximity to green space.

records.title: Spatial transitions as contrasts
records.summary: Treat spatial transitions as opportunities for contrasts, including dark lows leading to soaring heights and thresholds between secular and sacred.

evidence.quote: Treat spatial transitions as opportunities for contrasts: Dark lows leading to soaring heights—what Frank Lloyd Wright called “compression and release”; and thresholds between boundaries of the secular and the sacred or numinous, also known as 'thin spaces'.

records.title: Non-Visual Connection with Nature
records.summary: The Non-Visual Connection with Nature pattern aims to provide an environment that uses sound, scent, touch, temperature, and sometimes taste to reduce stress and improve physical and mental health.
actions: Design for non-visual connections that can be easily accessed and in ways that allow frequent engagement.
Design for multisensory connections to maximize potential positive health impact; non-visual connections enhance visual connections.
Prioritize nature sounds over mechanical masking and urban sounds.
Consider opportunities to integrate sensory experiences with architectural and mechanical solutions.

evidence.quote: • Design for non-visual connections that can be easily accessed and in ways that allow frequent engagement. • Design for multisensory connections to maximize potential positive health impact. Non-visual connections enhance visual connections. • Prioritize nature sounds over mechanical masking and urban sounds. • Consider opportunities to integrate sensory experiences with architectural and mechanical solutions.

records.title: Good Prospect is extensive and information rich
records.summary: Good Prospect is extensive and information rich, with a savanna–like view.

evidence.quote: Good Prospect is extensive and information rich, with a savanna–like view.

records.title: Use mid-range fractal dimension for digital designs
records.summary: When using digital algorithms, assign geometries with a mid-range dimensional ratio D=1.3–1.75.

evidence.quote: • Computer technology using the algorithms of mathematical and geometric functions can produce fractal designs with ease. If a digital approach is being taken, consider assigning geometries with a mid-range dimensional ratio (broadly speaking, D=1.3–1.75).

records.title: Prioritize real materials over synthetic
records.summary: Prioritize real natural materials over synthetic variations, because human brain receptors can distinguish between real and synthetic.

evidence.quote: • Prioritize real materials over synthetic variations. Human brain receptors can tell the difference between real and synthetic—which could make the difference between engendering intrigue or disappointment.

records.title: Orient seating to face nature
records.summary: Orient seating to face nature, rather than presenting nature as a backdrop.
constraints: nature should not be a backdrop

evidence.quote: Orient seating to face nature, rather than presenting nature as a backdrop.

records.title: Don't hide the grain
records.summary: Choose a finish that enhances the grain pattern of natural materials; avoid thick coats of paint, lacquer, or heavy polishing that hide natural characteristics.
actions: choose finish that enhances grain pattern

evidence.quote: • Don’t hide the grain. As the brain is more receptive to surface characteristics than form and shape, choose a finish that enhances the grain pattern—thick coats of paint or lacquer, or heavy polishing can hide the characteristics that distinguish it as being natural.

records.title: Preferred depth of field for mystery condition
records.summary: Greater visual access with a medium (≥20 ft) to high (≥100 ft) depth of field is preferred over shallow depth of field for a quality mystery condition.
prohibited_actions: shallow depth of field that leads to unpleasant surprises

evidence.quote: An obscured view with a shallow depth of field has shown to lead to unpleasant surprises, whereas greater visual access, with a medium (≥20 ft) to high (≥100 ft) depth of field is preferred (Herzog and Bryce, 2007).

records.title: Variable conductance materials and seating options
records.summary: Providing variable conductance materials and seating options with varying solar heat gain or proximity to operable windows can improve overall satisfaction.

evidence.quote: • Providing variable conductance materials, seating options with differing levels of solar heat gain or proximity to operable windows could improve the overall satisfaction of a space.

records.title: Avoid overuse
records.summary: Avoid the overuse of forms and patterns that may lead to visual toxicity.

evidence.quote: • Avoid the overuse of forms and patterns that may lead to visual toxicity.

records.title: Consider haptic and olfactory experiences
records.summary: Consider the haptic and olfactory experiences of a material or product to enhance the overall material connection.

evidence.quote: • Consider the haptic and olfactory experiences of a material or product to enhance the overall material connection.

records.title: Consider impact on existing fractal quality
records.summary: Consider the impact of new building or landscape design on the fractal quality of existing landscape or urban skyline.

evidence.quote: • Consider the impact of a new building or landscape design on the fractal quality of the existing landscape or urban skyline so as not to diminish existing benefits.

records.title: Prospect
records.summary: Visual preference research and spatial habitat responses have informed the evolution of this pattern, as well as cultural anthropology, evolutionary psychology and architectural analysis. Health benefits are suggested to include reduced stress, reduced boredom, irritation, fatigue, and perceived vulnerability, as well as improved comfort.

evidence.quote: [11] Prospect. Visual preference research and spatial habitat responses have informed the evolution of this pattern, as well as cultural anthropology, evolutionary psychology (e.g., Heerwagen & Orians, 1993) and architectural analysis (e.g., Dosen & Ostwald, 2013; Hildebrand, 1991; Appleton, 1996). Health benefits are suggested to include reduced stress (Grahn & Stigsdotter, 2010); reduced boredom, irritation, fatigue (Clearwater & Coss, 1991), and perceived vulnerability (Petherick, 2000; Wang & Taylor, 2006); as well as improved comfort (Herzog & Bryce, 2007).

records.title: Obscure boundaries and portion of focal subject
records.summary: A good mystery condition can be expressed through obscuring the boundaries and a portion of the focal subject, enticing the user to anticipate and explore.

evidence.quote: A good mystery condition could also be expressed through the obscuring of the boundaries and a portion of the focal subject (i.e., room, building, outdoor space, or other information source), thereby enticing the user to anticipate the full extent of the subject and explore the space further (Ikemi, 2005).

records.title: Balance fractal usage to avoid discomfort
records.summary: Avoid over-use or under-utilization of fractals; high-fractal dimensions can cause discomfort, while avoidance leads to predictability.

evidence.quote: • Find balance. Over-use of and/or extended exposure to high-fractal dimensions could instill discomfort or even fear, countering the intended response: to nourish and reduce stress, while avoidance or under-utilization of fractals could result in complete predictability and disinterest.

records.title: Prefer three iterations of fractals
records.summary: Fractal structures with iterations of three will be more impactful than designs with only two iterations.

evidence.quote: • Fractal structures with iterations of three will be more impactful than a design limited to two iterations.

records.title: Design Considerations for Prospect Condition
records.summary: Recommended design actions to create quality Prospect condition, including orienting building elements, providing focal lengths, limiting partition heights, locating stairwells at perimeter with glass, and elevating spaces with high ceilings.
actions: locate stairwells at building perimeter with glass façade and interior glass stairwell walls
elevate perimeter or interior spaces by 12–18 inches when high ceilings are present
constraints: focal length ≥ 20 feet (6 meters), preferably 100 feet (30 meters)

evidence.quote: Design considerations that may help create a quality Prospect condition: • Orienting building, fenestration, corridors and workstations will help optimize visual access to indoor or outdoor vistas, activity hubs or destinations.

Providing focal lengths of ≥20 feet (6 meters), preferably 100 feet (30 meters); when a space has sufficient depth, spatial properties can be leveraged to enhance the experience by removing visual barriers. Limiting partition heights to 42” will provide spatial barriers while allowing seated occupants to view across a space. Understory vegetation or hedges should use a similar guide; preferred height limitations will depend on terrain and how the space is most experienced (e.g., while sitting, standing, on a bicycle).
Locating stairwells at building perimeter with glass façade and interior glass stairwell walls can form a dual Prospect condition.
When high ceilings are present, perimeter or interior spaces elevated 12”–18” will enhance the Prospect condition.

records.title: Spatial condition for refuge: protection overhead and to the back
records.summary: The principal spatial condition is protection overhead and to one’s back, preferably on three sides.

evidence.quote: The principal spatial condition is protection overhead and to one’s back, preferably on three sides.

records.title: Broaden occupants' perception of thermal comfort
records.summary: Consider solutions that broaden occupants' perception of thermal comfort to increase acceptable temperature range and reduce energy demands.

evidence.quote: • Consider solutions that subsequently broaden the occupants' perception of thermal comfort, as it may also increase the range of acceptable temperatures and help reduce energy demands for air conditioning and heating.

records.title: Complexity & Order
records.summary: This pattern has evolved from research on fractal geometries and preferred views, perceptual and physiological stress responses to the complexity of fractals in nature, art and architecture, and the predictability of the occurrence of design in nature.

evidence.quote: [10] Complexity & Order. This pattern has evolved from research on fractal geometries and preferred views (Salingaros, 2012; Hägerhäll, Laike, Taylor et al., 2008; Hägerhäll, Purcella, & Taylor, 2004; Taylor, 2006); the perceptual and physiological stress responses to the complexity of fractals in nature, art and architecture (Salingaros, 2012; Joye, 2007; Taylor, 2006; S. Kaplan, 1988); and the predictability of the occurrence of design in nature (Bejan & Zane, 2012).

records.title: Incorporating airflow and thermal into ventilation/fenestration strategy
records.summary: Incorporate airflow and thermal conditions into strategy discussions for mechanical ventilation and/or fenestration to achieve distributed variability.

evidence.quote: • Incorporating airflow and thermal conditions into strategy discussions for mechanical ventilation and/or fenestration will help achieve distributed variability over space and time.

records.title: Maintain view lines when seated
records.summary: Design spatial layouts, partitions, and furnishings to uphold desired view lines and avoid impeding visual access when in a seated position.
constraints: avoid impeding visual access when seated

evidence.quote: Design spatial layouts, partitions, and furnishings to uphold desired view lines and avoid impeding the visual access when in a seated position.

records.title: Design for at least 5-20 minutes daily visual connection
records.summary: Design to support a visual connection that can be experienced for at least 5–20 minutes per day.
constraints: duration of visual connection should be at least 5-20 minutes per day

evidence.quote: Design to support a visual connection that can be experienced for at least 5-–20 minutes per day.

records.title: Prioritize fractal-based design elements
records.summary: Prioritize artwork, material selection, architectural expressions, landscape and master planning based on fractal geometries and hierarchies.

evidence.quote: • Prioritize artwork and material selection, architectural expressions, and landscape and master planning schemes based on fractal geometries and hierarchies.

records.title: Use awe-inspiring images when natural vistas unavailable
records.summary: When natural vistas are not possible, consider using awe-inspiring images of nature.
actions: consider using awe-inspiring images of nature
constraints: when vistas of sky, ocean, mountains or sunsets are not possible

evidence.quote: When vistas of sky, ocean, mountains or sunsets are not possible, consider awe-inspiring images of nature (Piff et al., 2015).

records.title: Incorporate places celebrating knowledge and mastery
records.summary: Incorporate places and programs that celebrate culture of knowledge, collaboration, skill mastery, and exploration of the unknown.
actions: incorporate places and programs that celebrate a culture of knowledge, collaboration, skill/craft mastery, and exploration or contemplation of the unknown

evidence.quote: Incorporate places and programs that celebrate a culture of knowledge, collaboration, skill/craft mastery, and the exploration or contemplation of the unknown.

records.title: Prefer distant prospect over short focal lengths
records.summary: Distant prospect (>100 ft, >30 m) is preferred for greater sense of awareness and comfort, especially when alone or in unfamiliar environments.

evidence.quote: Distant prospect (>100 feet, >30 meters) is preferred over shorter focal lengths (<20 feet, 6 meter) because it provides a greater sense of awareness and comfort, reducing one’s stress responses, particularly when alone or in unfamiliar environments (Petherick, 2000).

records.title: Prioritize real nature over simulated nature
records.summary: Prioritize real nature over simulated nature; and simulated nature over no nature.
constraints: simulated nature may be used when real nature is not feasible

evidence.quote: Prioritize real nature over simulated nature; and simulated nature over no nature. The benefits of viewing real nature may be attenuated by a digital medium, which may be of greatest value to spaces that, due to the nature of its function (e.g., hospital radiation units) cannot easily incorporate real nature or views to the outdoors.

records.title: Even small instances of nature can be restorative
records.summary: Even small instances of nature can be restorative, and particularly relevant for temporary interventions or spaces with limited real estate.

evidence.quote: Even small instances of nature can be restorative, and particularly relevant for temporary interventions, or spaces where real estate (floor/ground area, wall space) is limited.

records.title: Non-Rhythmic Sensory Stimuli Design Guideline
records.summary: As a general guideline, sensory experiences should occur non-rhythmically but preferably not more than 20 minutes apart, for a duration of 20 seconds or more and, to induce eye fatigue restoration, from a distance of more than 100 feet.

evidence.quote: As a general guideline, sensory experiences should occur non-rhythmically but preferably not more than 20 minutes apart, for a duration of 20 seconds or more and, to induce eye fatigue restoration, from a distance of more than 100 feet.

records.title: Objective of Thermal & Airflow Variability
records.summary: Provide an environment that allows users to experience airflow variability and thermal variability, and enable user control over thermal conditions.

evidence.quote: The objective of the Thermal & Airflow Variability pattern is to provide an environment that allows users to experience the sensory elements of airflow variability and thermal variability. The intent is also for the user/occupant to be able to control thermal conditions, either by using individual controls, or by having access to variable conditions within a space or series of spaces.

records.title: Light levels in refuge spaces should differ from adjacent spaces
records.summary: Light levels in refuge spaces should differ from adjacent spaces and user lighting controls will broaden functionality as a refuge space.
constraints: light levels should differ from adjacent spaces

evidence.quote: Light levels in refuge spaces should differ from adjacent spaces and user lighting controls will broaden functionality as a refuge space.

records.title: Early intervention cost effectiveness
records.summary: More comprehensive interventions will be more cost effective when introduced early in the design process.

evidence.quote: • More comprehensive interventions will be more cost effective when they are introduced early in the design process.

records.title: Apply on multiple planes
records.summary: Apply biomorphic forms and patterns on 2 or 3 planes or dimensions for greater diversity and frequency of exposure.
constraints: for greater diversity and frequency of exposure

evidence.quote:
Design considerations that may help create a quality biomorphic condition:
• Apply on 2 or 3 planes or dimensions (e.g., floor plane and wall; furniture windows and soffits) for greater diversity and frequency of exposure.

records.title: Early coordination among project team disciplines
records.summary: Coordinate design strategies among project team disciplines as early as the schematic design process to achieve the experiential outcome.
constraints: as early as the schematic design process

evidence.quote: • Coordination of design strategies among project team disciplines (e.g., architect, lighting designer, MEP engineers) as early as the schematic design process is critical for achieving this experiential outcome.

records.title: Provide multiple types of refuge spaces
records.summary: To address varying needs, provide more than one kind of refuge space through differing spatial dimensions, lighting conditions, and degree of concealment.

evidence.quote: To address varying needs, preferences and acitivies, provide more than one kind of refuge space through differing spatial dimensions, lighting conditions, and degree of concealment.
